items.q: When energy is 'lost' as heat due to friction, what has really happened?
items.correct: Energy has been transferred to the thermal store of the surroundings — it's not destroyed, just dissipated.
items.misconception: Energy has been used up and no longer exists.
items.random_wrong: Energy has been converted into mass following E=mc^2.
items.why: Everyday language ('used up', 'lost') and bills ('energy used') reinforce the idea energy is consumed, contradicting conservation.

items.q: What is the difference between energy and power?
items.correct: Energy is measured in joules; power is the rate of energy transfer, measured in watts (joules per second).
items.misconception: Energy and power are the same — both describe how much fuel something uses.
items.random_wrong: Power is the energy used over the lifetime of a device.
items.why: Students use 'power' in everyday language to mean 'a lot of energy', missing that power has the dimension of energy per time.

items.q: If a car doubles its speed, what happens to its kinetic energy?
items.correct: It quadruples (because KE = 1/2 mv^2 — kinetic energy is proportional to v^2).
items.misconception: Kinetic energy doubles because energy is proportional to speed.
items.random_wrong: Kinetic energy stays the same because mass hasn't changed.
items.why: Students assume linear relationships (double the input, double the output) and forget the v^2 factor in the kinetic energy formula.
The answer examiners want is: wind. “coal” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Bulb input 60 W, useful light 9 W. Efficiency (%)?
items.why: The real figure is 15. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
